| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_IJB_05    | What was the financial result for Moray's integrated health and social care body in 2022/23?                                                  | [22]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0005_001       | in Scotland by general funding allocation. A new operating model aimed at strengthening integration between hospital and community based services was introduced in shadow form in April 2021 and subsequently endorsed by the Board in August 2021. Services are delivered based on a portfolio management structure covering Family services, Surgical and Clinical Support Services, Medical and Unscheduled Care, Mental Health and wellbeing, Population Health and services in Moray. The portfolio arrangements also include, a range of Primary and Community Care services delegated to the three Integration Joint Boards (IJBs) covering Aberdeen City, Aberdeenshire and Moray. All our direct clinical services are supported by corporate functions such as Facilities, E- Health, Finance and Human Resources. The IJBs are legally constituted cross-sector bodies providing a focus for NHS Grampian to work in partnership with other agencies, including Local Authorities and Primary Healthcare contractors, in the provision of integrated services to the local communities. Executive Director and Non-Executive members of the NHS Grampian Board are members of the Integration Joint Boards which provide the strategic direction for the integration of adult health and social care.                                       | [5]         | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | A) OVERVIEW                                                                                    |            5 |

| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_AE_01     | How many patients attended an Emergency Department in Grampian during March 2023?                                                             | [10]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0010_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 2. Non-Financial Performance Summary 2.1 Key National Treatment Targets The impact of these emergency measures on the key national treatment targets is summarised below: Attendance at Accident & Emergency 98% of patients should wait no more than 4 hours from arrival to admission, discharge or transfer for accident and emergency treatment. Performance Total A&E attendances in March 2023 were 9,767, an increase of 1.9% over March 2022. ED attendance rates per 100,000 population in NHS Grampian are consistently among the lowest in Scotland. The percentage seen within 4 hours in an A&E department in March was 65.2% (71.8% for March 2022) compared to the Scotland wide rate of 68% (71.6% March 2022). The number who waited over 4 hours in March 2023 was 3,396, of whom 250 waited over 12 hours, compared to 2,698 (178 over 12 hours) in March 2022                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             | [10]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           10 |

| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_DEL_01    | How many patients at the March 2023 census were waiting longer than the standard threshold before discharge from hospital?                    | [11]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0009_001       | workforce challenges alongside growing financial pressures, the Scottish Government have clarified the areas of activity to be prioritised during the coming year as a phased reduction in waiting times with no outpatient waiting longer than one year by March 2023 and no-one waiting longer than one year for in- patient/day case treatment by September 2024. A proposed plan to achieve reduction in current waiting times through investment in a range of initiatives is under consideration by the Scottish Government. | Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_DEL_02    | How many days were patients spending in hospital due to delayed discharge in Grampian in March 2023?                                          | [12]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0012_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Patients spent 2,575 days in hospital due to delays in discharge in Grampian in March 2023 (3,143 in March 2022). Treatment Time Guarantee 12 week in-patient / daycase Treatment Time Guarantee (TTG) and 12 weeks for first outpatient appointment. Performance Available capacity is targeted at urgent and priority patients (Elective Surgery Category System (ESCatS) 0 and 1 including cancer) and to address long waiting patients. The tables below set out the increases in the number of patients waiting for in- patient/daycase treatment and a first outpatient appointment. The proportion of patients waiting over 12 weeks for in-patient/daycase treatment was 78.2% at 31 March 2023 (72.1% at 31 March 2022) 49.2% of patients on the new outpatient list were waiting over 12 weeks at 31 March 2023 (48.4% at 31 March 2022)                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                            | [12]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           12 |
| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_DEL_P1    | How did delayed discharges in March 2023 compare to March 2022?                                                                               | [11]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0011_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS The pressures on Emergency Departments (ED) during the year continue to be well publicised across the NHS in Scotland and the UK. Our local analysis suggests that the ED performance was acting as the bellwether of the pressures experienced right across the whole health and social care system with capacity and flow factors affecting each step in a patients pathway combining to manifest at the front door of the hospital affecting the 4-hour performance. Delayed Discharge Reduction in the number of patients awaiting discharge from hospital into a more appropriate care setting, once treatment is complete (standard and code 9 delay reasons). Performance There were 72 patients, of which 70 were more than 3 days delayed at the March 2023 census date (104 at March 2022, of which 91 were delayed more than 3 days). Of the mainland Boards, NHS Grampian consistently has one of the lowest number of standard and code 9 delays per 100,000 population.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         | [11]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           11 |
| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_GOV_01    | Were all three of NHS Grampian's statutory financial obligations met in 2022/23?                                                              | [23]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0023_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 3.2 Grampian Health Board performance against statutory financial targets The results of each of the partner organisations, although consolidated with the main Board accounts for group accounting purposes, do not form part of the statutory financial target set for NHS Boards by the Scottish Government Health and Social Care Directorates (SGHSCD) and are, therefore, not taken in to account when considering the rmance. NHS Boards have the ability to manage small under/overspends (within 1% of resource) within an overall breakeven position across a three year period (2022-25). The Scottish Government require NHS Boards to meet three key financial targets: Revenue Resource Limit (RRL) a resource budget for ongoing activity; Capital Resource Limit (CRL) a resource budget for net capital investment; and Cash Requirement a financing requirement to fund the cash consequences of ongoing activity and net capital investment. Health Boards are required to contain their net expenditure within these limits, and report on any variation. Grampian Health Board has successfully achieved all three financial targets for the year reporting an out                                                                                                                       | [23]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           23 |
| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_GOV_02    | What financial planning framework did NHS Grampian operate under in 2022/23?                                                                  | [24]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0024_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Financial flexibility: funding banked with/(provided by) 0 Scottish Government Underlying Surplus against Core Revenue Resource Limit 612 0% Further details on non-core elements of expenditure, typically comprising items of a technical accounting nature, can be found in the Summary of Resource Outturn. The Board is reporting an underspend of £0.612 million against a target of breakeven on the revenue resource limit for 2022/23. A one year financial plan was submitted to Scottish Government by NHS Grampian on 21 March 2022. Due to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, the normal Scottish Government three year Annual Operating and financial planning process was amended. NHS Boards were required to submit three year financial plans to the Scottish Government in August 2022 covering the period April 2022 - March 2025. Subsequently there has been a return of formal three year financial planning from 2023-24 with an updated financial plan submitted to the Scottish Government in March 2023 covering the period 2023 - 2026. The                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | [24]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           24 |
| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_FIN_09    | What was the total COVID-19 expenditure across NHS Grampian and its IJBs in 2022/23?                                                          | [25]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | table             | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'table', 'prefer_table_chunks': True}      |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0020_001       | /22 2020/21 Restated Total expenditure for the year was £4.002 million (2021/22 £2.476 million), £3.471 million relating to grants for charitable activities (2021/22 £2.050 million), £0.334 million on staffing and support costs (2021/22 £0.203 million) and £0.197 million on investment management fees (2021/22 £0.223 million). The net effect was a surplus of income over expenditure for the year of £0.110 million. The charity will continue to work closely with all relevant Health and related services as they re- mobilise and return to a more normal footing, to ensure that available funds are utilised effectively, improving the health and wellbeing of patients and staff in line with the wishes of our donors. 3.1.2 Integration Joint Boards (IJBs) - ordination and collaboration of the response and remobilisation from the COVID-19 pandemic.                |            |                     |                 |                    |                          |                                    | 150 | Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_FIN_P2    | By how much did drugs and medical supplies increase in 2022/23 and what drove this?                                                           | [25]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0025_001       | £1,443 million (2021/22 £1,453 million), a decrease of £11 million over the prior year. The Consolidated Statement of Comprehensive Net Expenditure can be found on Page 85, a more detailed analysis of Expenditure in note 3 on Page 114 and a more detailed analysis of income in note 4 on Page 115. Staff Costs increased by £54.7 million mainly due to the annual pay uplift (2021/22 £56 million) which was an average of 4.5% for Medical & Dental staff and 7.5% for Agenda for Change staff and national insurance increases (£4 million), partially offset by a reduction in the annual leave accrual (2021/22 £8.4 million) as staff were able to utilise leave following the pandemic. Drugs and Medical supplies increased by £21 million mainly due to volume and price increases across both primary and secondary care sector. These were partially offset by a reduction in medical supply spend on COVID response activity £6.5 million. Private sector expenditure activity to support waiting times plan increases                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                | [25]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           25 |
| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_PLAN_01   | Did the 2022/23 financial plan present a break-even position?                                                                                 | [24]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0024_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Financial flexibility: funding banked with/(provided by) 0 Scottish Government Underlying Surplus against Core Revenue Resource Limit 612 0% Further details on non-core elements of expenditure, typically comprising items of a technical accounting nature, can be found in the Summary of Resource Outturn. The Board is reporting an underspend of £0.612 million against a target of breakeven on the revenue resource limit for 2022/23. A one year financial plan was submitted to Scottish Government by NHS Grampian on 21 March 2022. Due to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, the normal Scottish Government three year Annual Operating and financial planning process was amended. NHS Boards were required to submit three year financial plans to the Scottish Government in August 2022 covering the period April 2022 - March 2025. Subsequently there has been a return of formal three year financial planning from 2023-24 with an updated financial plan submitted to the Scottish Government in March 2023 covering the period 2023 - 2026. The                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | [24]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           24 |

| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_AE_P2     | What was the Scotland-wide A&E 4-hour performance rate in March 2023?                                                                         | [10]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0010_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 2. Non-Financial Performance Summary 2.1 Key National Treatment Targets The impact of these emergency measures on the key national treatment targets is summarised below: Attendance at Accident & Emergency 98% of patients should wait no more than 4 hours from arrival to admission, discharge or transfer for accident and emergency treatment. Performance Total A&E attendances in March 2023 were 9,767, an increase of 1.9% over March 2022. ED attendance rates per 100,000 population in NHS Grampian are consistently among the lowest in Scotland. The percentage seen within 4 hours in an A&E department in March was 65.2% (71.8% for March 2022) compared to the Scotland wide rate of 68% (71.6% March 2022). The number who waited over 4 hours in March 2023 was 3,396, of whom 250 waited over 12 hours, compared to 2,698 (178 over 12 hours) in March 2022                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                             | [10]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           10 |
| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_PLAN_02   | What value and sustainability programme was initiated in 2022/23?                                                                             | [4]              | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | A OVERVIEW                    | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0027_001       | 90. 3.2.6 Pension Liabilities The accounting policy for pensions is provided in Note 1 to the Accounts and disclosure of the costs is shown within Note 19 and the Remuneration Report. 3.2.7 Infrastructure and Non-Current Assets The Board delivered a programme of infrastructure investment totalling £109.7 million during 2022/23. Within this, a total of £105.605 million of capital expenditure was incurred (see note 7d) and a further £4.05 million was classed as revenue expenditure in nature. The overall programme was resourced using a combination of capital funding from the SGHSCD (£104.3 million, see note 7d), income from donations (£0.3 million), financial transactions funding from the Scottish Government for a GP sustainability Loan (£0.7 million), NBV benefit from disposal of equipment (£0.26 million) and various revenue funding sources (£4.05 million) Major investments during the year include: Ongoing Construction of the Baird Family Hospital and the ANCHOR Cancer Centre (£85                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                       | [27]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           27 |
| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_FIN_11    | What was the Non-Core Revenue Resource Limit for 2022/23?                                                                                     | [23]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | table             | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'table', 'prefer_table_chunks': True}      |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0023_001       | and Cash Requirement a financing requirement to fund the cash consequences of ongoing activity and net capital investment. Health Boards are required to contain their net expenditure within these limits, and report on any variation. Grampian Health Board has successfully achieved all three financial targets for the year reporting an outturn against these set limits as follows: Statutory Financial Targets Limit as set Actual Variance by SGHSCD Outturn (Deficit)/Sur plus Core Revenue Resource Limit 1,292,491 1,291,879 612 Non-Core Revenue Resource Limit 46,200 46,200 - Total Revenue Resource Limit 1,338,691 1,338,079 612 Core Capital Resource Limit 104,268 104,268 - Non-Core Capital Resource Limit 1,068 1,068 - Total Capital Resource Limit 105,336 105,336 - Cash Requirement 1,546,903 1,546,903 - Memorandum for In Year Out-turn £000                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | [23]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           23 |
| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_GOV_P1    | What was the change in the NHS Grampian financial planning regime between 2022/23 and earlier years?                                          | [24]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0024_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Financial flexibility: funding banked with/(provided by) 0 Scottish Government Underlying Surplus against Core Revenue Resource Limit 612 0% Further details on non-core elements of expenditure, typically comprising items of a technical accounting nature, can be found in the Summary of Resource Outturn. The Board is reporting an underspend of £0.612 million against a target of breakeven on the revenue resource limit for 2022/23. A one year financial plan was submitted to Scottish Government by NHS Grampian on 21 March 2022. Due to the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic, the normal Scottish Government three year Annual Operating and financial planning process was amended. NHS Boards were required to submit three year financial plans to the Scottish Government in August 2022 covering the period April 2022 - March 2025. Subsequently there has been a return of formal three year financial planning from 2023-24 with an updated financial plan submitted to the Scottish Government in March 2023 covering the period 2023 - 2026. The                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | [24]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           24 |
| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_SAV_P2    | By how much did NHS Grampian exceed its efficiency savings target in 2022/23?                                                                 | [24]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0024_001       | April 2022 - March 2025. Subsequently there has been a return of formal three year financial planning from 2023-24 with an updated financial plan submitted to the Scottish Government in March 2023 covering the period 2023 - 2026. The Board has also updated the five year Medium Term Financial Strategy. The Core Revenue Resource Limit for 2022/23 was also adjusted to include the £0.131 million surplus carried forward from 2021/22. 3.2.1 Efficiency Savings Efficiency savings of £10.2 million were achieved against a target of £5.4 million for NHS Grampian services. The main savings achieved were in the areas of travel costs, review of old orders, reduction of untaken annual leave accrual, procurement, cross boundary charges and skill mix in non-clinical areas. Recurring savings amounted to £2.5 million and non-recurring of £7.7 million. 3.2.2 Financial impact of the COVID-19 Pandemic The impact of the pandemic was less severe on services in 202                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | [24]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           24 |

| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_FIN_16    | What was the COVID-19 vaccination expenditure in 2022/23?                                                                                     | [25]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0024_002       | in non-clinical areas. Recurring savings amounted to £2.5 million and non-recurring of £7.7 million. 3.2.2 Financial impact of the COVID-19 Pandemic The impact of the pandemic was less severe on services in 2022/23 than in 2021/22 but significant costs were still incurred across a range of services, including the COVID vaccination programme and the provision of sustainability payments to social care providers. Expenditure of £23.1 million was incurred on non-delegated Board services. The main areas incurring additional costs were in additional Personal Protective equipment, vaccinations programmes, testing and contact tracing and staffing and other costs to support infection control measures. The level of expenditure was fully funded by the Scottish Government. 0 million (2021/22 £62.4 million). The largest element of this was on support payments for Social Care providers. The expenditure was funded from the earmarked Covid reserve held by                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                               | [24]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           24 |
| Grampian-2022-2023 | Q_2023_GOV_05    | What programme did NHS Grampian initiate in 2022/23 to address long-term financial sustainability?                                            | [4]              | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | A OVERVIEW                    | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2022-2023:p0004_001       | level of care in our communities and build on the excellent foundations established through the integration of health and social care. Our operational performance has been impacted by the health debt generated over the last three years, however, I am pleased that during the financial year 2022/23 we have been able to balance our revenue budget using one-off income and savings. This income and savings will unfortunately not be available in future years and I do not underestimate the challenge facing NHS Grampian in balancing future budgets. In this regard a significant amount of work has been undertaken during the last quarter of 2022/23 to prepare a local value and sustainability programme. The purpose of this programme is to develop and deliver short term efficiency savings and over the medium to longer term work on how we make sustainable services and pathways of care. During this period of preparing for the future your input and support will be important, and I would thank you in advance for working with colleagues and services as we make the necessary changes over the coming months. It is a privilege to be the Chief Executive at this time and I am incredibly grateful for your support. | Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_FIN_05    | How much brokerage did Scottish Government provide NHS Grampian in 2023/24?                                                                   | [27]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0039_000       | A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT i) THE DIRECTORS’ REPORT CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT THE DIRECTORS’ REPORT 1. Naming Convention NHS Grampian is the common name of Grampian Health Board. NHS Grampian Charity is the common name for the Grampian Health Board Endowment Funds. 2. Date of Issue The Accountable Officer authorised these financial statements for issue on 28 June 2024. 3. Going Concern After making enquiries, the Board has a reasonable expectation that the organisation has adequate resources to continue in operational existence for the foreseeable future. The Board expects to record a further revenue deficit in the 2024/25 financial year but is planning for the level of deficit to reduce over the following subsequent financial years as outlined in the Medium Term Financial Plan. Discussions will continue with the Scottish Government over the provision of further funding cover (known as “brokerage”) to support the projected revenue deficit. It is expected NHS Board will continue in operational existence for the foreseeable future and continue to provide services, with no indication by Scottish Ministers of any intention for dissolution without transfer                                                                                                                                  | [39]        | ACCOUNTABILITY REPORT              | A) CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT                                                                 |           39 |

| Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_FIN_09    | What was the projected deficit in the financial plan submitted in March 2023 before additional funding was confirmed?                         | [27]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0027_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS Memorandum for In Year Out-turn £000 Core Revenue Resource Variance Surplus in 2023/24 97 Financial flexibility: funding banked with/(provided by) -24,800 Scottish Government Underlying Deficit against Core Revenue Resource Limit -24,703 1.66% Further details on non-core elements of expenditure, typically comprising items of a technical accounting nature, can be found in the Summary of Resource Outturn. NHS Grampian was not able to set a balanced revenue budget for the 2023/24 financial year. The financial plan submitted to Scottish Government in March 2023 projected a £60.627 million deficit for the year after assuming savings achievement for the year of £16.557 million. Scottish Government confirmed additional funding for Health Boards in May which reduced the projected deficit to £42.936 million and further additional funding in February which has reduced the projected deficit to £25.103 million. The deficit highlights the level of financial challenge experienced during the year driven mainly by a combination of continuing service pressures and high levels of general inflation. Diff                                                                                                                                                                | [27]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           27 |
| Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_FIN_10    | By what amount did drugs and medical supplies expenditure increase in 2023/24?                                                                | [29]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0029_001       | which was an average between 6-12% for Medical & Dental staff and 6.5% for Agenda for Change staff alongside national insurance increases. These were offset by a reduction in the accrual for untaken annual leave of £8.24 million (2022/23 £5.6 million) System pressures resulted in additional expenditure to support staffing levels with spend on agency locum costs increasing by £2.7 million, agency nursing costs by £2.7 million and £2 million increase in supplementary payments for non-compliant junior medical rotas. Drugs and Medical supplies increased by £28.3 million (£21 million 2022/23) due to price and volume increases. This impacted both the primary and secondary care sectors and reflected the volatility in the pricing of medicines in the year along with new treatment pathways. Medical specialties within secondary care were particularly impacted due to activity growth. Increases in expenditure was also experienced in the independent primary care sector of £11.8m due to increases in activity and uplifts to national contract values. Increases in other non-pay costs for the year of £7                                                                                                                                                                                                           | [29]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           29 |
| Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_FIN_11    | What was NHS Grampian's clinical negligence and employer liability provision at year end 2023/24?                                             | [30]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0116_000       | concerning the future. The resulting accounting estimates will, by definition, seldom equal the related actual results. The Board makes judgements in applying accounting policies. The estimates, assumptions and judgements that have a significant risk of causing a material adjustment to the carrying amounts of assets and liabilities within the financial statements within the next financial year are addressed below. Clinical and Medical Negligence Provision: The clinical and medical negligence provision is calculated using information received from the Central Legal Office regarding claims they have received relating to NHS Grampian. The provision covers all claims classified as category 3 and 50% of the value of claims in category 2 which have been assessed as having a probability of settlement. The share of the NHS Scotland CNORIS liability is estimated based on actual settlement trends in prior years. The carrying value of both the provision and receivable is as shown below: 2023/24 2022/23* £000’s £000’s Provision recognising individual claims 175,012 154,579 against the NHS Board at 31 March (*) Associated CNORIS receivable at 31                                                                                                                                                          | [116]       | FINANCIAL STATEMENTS               | Unknown                                                                                        |          116 |

| Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_FIN_13    | How many directly employed staff did NHS Grampian have in 2023/24?                                                                            | [5]              | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | A OVERVIEW                    | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0084_002       | for employment from disabled persons and actively seeks to provide an environment where they and any employees who become disabled can continue to contribute to the work of the Board. During 2023/24 NHS Grampian employed an average 76.1WTE (headcount 92) staff with a disability compared to 71.8WTE (headcount 102) in 2022/23. The NHS Grampian Equality, Diversity and Human Rights Policy can be accessed at the following link https://www.nhsgrampian.org/about-us/equality-and-diversity/. This covers, in detail, the employment of disabled persons including recruitment and selection, treatment during employment and monitoring arrangements. The policy also lists the other NHS Grampian Policies which are in place to ensure that applicants for posts with a disability and staff in post with a disability are treated fairly and that everything reasonably possible is done to meet their needs. This includes providing appropriate support and making reasonable adjustments, training, career development and promotion of disabled persons. 7. | Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_SAV_01    | What efficiency savings did NHS Grampian achieve in 2023/24 against its target?                                                               | [28]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0028_001       | entity with associated funding arrangements. 3.2.1 Delivering Sustainable Healthcare – Finding Balance This was the first time that NHS Grampian has overspent against its revenue budget and highlights the severe pressures that the health system faces in the North East of Scotland. A number of actions were taken to combat this overspend including the development in the year. These included amongst other measures: Development of an Integrated Performance Assurance Framework - Vacancy Controls - Review and control agency nursing and locum spend - Efficiency savings programme - Value and sustainability programme - 3.2.2 Efficiency Savings Efficiency savings of £17.52 million (2022/23 £10.2 million) were achieved against a target of £16.51 million (2022/23 £5.4 million) for NHS Grampian services. The scale of these savings was significantly higher than in the previous year and the delivery of these savings was supported by the development of an assurance and performance framework. A Programme Management Office was implemented to support this along with other elements of value and sustainability work. | Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_AE_01     | What were the total A&E attendances in March 2024 and how did they compare to March 2023?                                                     | [12]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0012_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS 2. Non-Financial Performance Summary 2.1 Key National Treatment Targets Our performance against the key national treatment targets is summarised below: Attendance at Accident & Emergency 98% of patients should wait no more than 4 hours from arrival to admission, discharge or transfer for accident and emergency treatment. Performance - Total A&E attendances in March 2024 were 10,878, an increase of 11.4% over March 2023. - ED attendance rates per 100,000 population in NHS Grampian remain consistently among the lowest in Scotland. - The percentage seen within 4 hours in an A&E department in March was 67.8% (65.2% for March 2023) compared to the Scotland wide rate of 67.4% (68% March 2023). - The number who waited over 4 hours in March 2024 was 3,478 of whom 370 waited over 12 hours, compared to 3,396 (250 over 12 hours) in March 2023.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                  | [12]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           12 |

The Plan is structured around People, Places and Pathways, with priority areas aligned to each of these strands, with a central overarching objective to balance enabling wellness with responding to illness, as illustrated in the following graphic: In support of this, a Three Year delivery plan 2023-2026 was agreed by the Board in August 2023 as part of a single coherent plan including the Annual Delivery Plan and Medium Term Financial Plan for NHS Grampian. This Delivery plan sets out how NHS Grampian will make progress against the priority areas and strategic intent within the Plan for the Future for 2023 – 2026 in response to the 10 recovery priority areas of the Scottish Government. | Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_ESC_01    | To which escalation stage was NHS Grampian elevated in November 2023 and why?                                                                 | [4, 27]          | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | A OVERVIEW                    | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0027_002       | repayable to the Scottish Government at the point in the future when the Board returns to financial balance. Without this additional support, the Board’s final outturn would have been an overspend of £24.703 million (equivalent to 1.66% of the Revenue Resource Limit). A surplus of £0.97 million against the Core Revenue Resource Limit is reported for 2023/24 reflecting this financial support. In November 2023, Scottish Government also informed NHS Grampian that the Board had been escalated from Stage 1 to Stage 2 of the NHS Scotland Support and Intervention Framework as a result of the Board’s financial position. This means that scheduled engagement and review of performance between the Scottish Government and the Board will continue through the usual channels. NHS Grampian submitted its 2024/25 Financial Plan to the Scottish Government in March 2024. The plan does not present a break-even position, recognising the significant work to                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                     | [27]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           27 |
| Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_ESC_02    | What was the underlying reason for NHS Grampian's escalation in 2023/24?                                                                      | [4, 27]          | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | A OVERVIEW                    | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0058_000       | A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT considered to be in the best interest of patients, carers, the public, staff members and the Board. To support the effective assessment and management of risk within NHS Grampian, a Risk Appetite Statement is used to communicate the amount and type of risk that the organisation aims to seek, accept or tolerate in pursuit of achieving its strategic objectives. Subsequent to Board Seminars focusing on Risk Appetite on 12th January 2023 and 6th July 2023, the Board endorsed the organisation’s updated Risk Appetite Statement on the 5th October 2023. The Risk Appetite Statement is reviewed on an annual basis, or if there are any significant changes to the operating environment. 6.2 Escalation and Assurance for Strategic Risk NHS Grampian’s Strategic Risk Register aims to identify and articulate significant risk that has the potential to impact upon the achievement of the organisation’s strategic objectives, as outlined in Plan for the Future. The effective identification and management of the Board’s Strategic Risks aids assurance that activities and the prioritisation of resources are                                                                                                                                                                                 | [58]        | ACCOUNTABILITY REPORT              | A) CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT                                                                 |           58 |
| Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_ESC_03    | What risk-based approach did the Chief Executive Team implement to manage the financial challenge in 2023/24?                                 | [4]              | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | A OVERVIEW                    | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0004_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW The financial challenges NHS Grampian faced in 2023/24 are highlighted in the Annual Report and Accounts for this year. The Accounts show a small underspend against the revenue budget, but this was only achieved due to additional repayable financial support (known as “brokerage”) being provided to the Board by the Scottish Government. This highlights the severe financial pressures currently being faced by the health system in the North East. This challenging financial position is anticipated to continue into future years. The Chief Executive Team of the Board have been working to implement a risk based approach to managing this challenge which involves achieving an appropriate balance between our financial position, clinical governance matters, staff wellbeing and prevention. During the year a significant amount of work has been undertaken to further develop and implement a local value and sustainability programme engaging widely with colleagues across the system. The purpose of this programme is to deliver short term efficiency savings and over the medium to longer term work on how we transform services and pathways of care to make them sustainable in terms of quality of care, staffing and finance. I recognise the significant financial and workforce    | [4]         | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | A) OVERVIEW                                                                                    |            4 |
| Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_ESC_04    | What is the Grampian Operational Pressure Escalation System (G-OPES) and was it used in 2023/24?                                              | [56]             | ACCOUNTABILITY REPORT | A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0056_000       | A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT 4.3.2 The Grampian Operational Pressure Escalation System (G-OPES) The G-OPES model to escalate responses to demand pressures in the system continued to be used throughout 2023/24. G-OPES provides: A single approach to evaluate pressure across the health and care system A system view focusing on pathways of care supported by Portfolio leadership - Options to increase capacity through risk/pressure-based derogations of some - standards A whole system approach to share risk and offer internal mutual aid - Clarity for staff - Transparency - Specific protection of time-critical planned care - G-OPES includes at its highest levels a series of derogations to normal practice, which require decisions balancing three dimensions of quality of care against each other and using a risk and ethical based approach to reach a better overall position. Decisions about the derogations applied using G-OPES are taken by the system leadership. The pathways in G-OPES were reviewed in the year to ensure that they remain fit and aligned with Business Continuity and Service recovery plans to                                                                                                                                                                                                | [56]        | ACCOUNTABILITY REPORT              | A) CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT                                                                 |           56 |
| Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_GOPES_01  | What review of G-OPES pathways was undertaken in 2023/24?                                                                                     | [56]             | ACCOUNTABILITY REPORT | A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0056_000       | A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT 4.3.2 The Grampian Operational Pressure Escalation System (G-OPES) The G-OPES model to escalate responses to demand pressures in the system continued to be used throughout 2023/24. G-OPES provides: A single approach to evaluate pressure across the health and care system A system view focusing on pathways of care supported by Portfolio leadership - Options to increase capacity through risk/pressure-based derogations of some - standards A whole system approach to share risk and offer internal mutual aid - Clarity for staff - Transparency - Specific protection of time-critical planned care - G-OPES includes at its highest levels a series of derogations to normal practice, which require decisions balancing three dimensions of quality of care against each other and using a risk and ethical based approach to reach a better overall position. Decisions about the derogations applied using G-OPES are taken by the system leadership. | Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_GOV_02    | What actions were taken in 2023/24 to combat the financial overspend?                                                                         | [28]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0028_001       | entity with associated funding arrangements. 3.2.1 Delivering Sustainable Healthcare – Finding Balance This was the first time that NHS Grampian has overspent against its revenue budget and highlights the severe pressures that the health system faces in the North East of Scotland. A number of actions were taken to combat this overspend including the development in the year. These included amongst other measures: Development of an Integrated Performance Assurance Framework - Vacancy Controls - Review and control agency nursing and locum spend - Efficiency savings programme - Value and sustainability programme - 3.2.2 Efficiency Savings Efficiency savings of £17.52 million (2022/23 £10.2 million) were achieved against a target of £16.51 million (2022/23 £5.4 million) for NHS Grampian services. The scale of these savings was significantly higher than in the previous year and the delivery of these savings was supported by the development of an assurance and performance framework. A Programme Management Office was implemented to support this along with other elements of value and sustainability work. This includes participation in the bi-annual National Fraud Initiative which is a data matching exercise. The Board also takes part in a post payment patient exemption eligibility verification system, which covers all Family Health Service expenditure. 5. Payment Policy NHS Grampian is committed to supporting the Scottish Government in helping businesses during the current economic situation by paying bills more quickly. The intention is to achieve payment of all undisputed invoices within 10 working days, across all public bodies and NHS Grampian is actively working towards achievement of this accelerated payment target. - In 2023/24, the average credit taken was 14 days (2022/23 15 days). - In 2023/24 93% of invoices by value (2022/23 91%) and 90% of invoices by volume (2022/23 86%) were paid within 30 days. - In 2023/24 87% of invoices by value (2022/23 85%) and                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                              | [33]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           33 |
| Grampian-2023-2024 | Q_2024_GOV_05    | What infrastructure investment programme did NHS Grampian deliver in 2023/24?                                                                 | [31]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2023-2024:p0031_002       | Assets The Board delivered a programme of infrastructure investment totalling £121.3 million during 2023/24. Within this, a total of £117.442 million of capital expenditure was incurred (see note 7d) and a further £3.87 million was classed as revenue expenditure in nature. The overall programme was resourced using a combination of capital funding from the SGHSCD (£113.6 million, see note 7d), income from donations (£1.25 million), financial transactions funding from the Scottish Government for GP sustainability Loans (£2.24 million), NBV benefit from disposal of equipment (£0.31 million) and various revenue funding sources (£3.87 million) Major investments during the year include: - Ongoing Construction of the Baird Family Hospital and the ANCHOR Cancer Centre (£95.9 million). The Baird Family Hospital will replace the existing maternity hospital, breast screening, gynaecology, and neo-natal facilities. The Anchor Cancer centre will be located adjacent to the existing radiotherapy building on the Aberdeen Royal Infirmary site. The main savings achieved in addition to technical adjustments (£28.6m) were in the areas of agency spend including medical locum and nursing costs, hospital drug costs from switching from branded medicines to generic alternatives, staff cost control measures including vacancy freeze and reduction of overtime, procurement and increased charges for catering. Recurring savings amounted to £25.2 million (2023/24 £12.38 million) and non-recurring of £25.3 million (2023/24 £5.14 million). 3.2.3 Expenditure Analysis Net expenditure for the year was £1,681 million (2023/24 £1,565 million), an increase of £116 million from the prior year. | Grampian-2024-2025 | Q_2025_SAV_01    | What cost reduction obligation did NHS Grampian face for 2024/25, and by how much was it exceeded?                                            | [31]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2024-2025:p0043_001       | to the Scottish Government. However, for this year's Annual Accounts report, we are unable to provide a comprehensive account of all emissions. This is due to the reliance on data from national bodies such as the Scottish Government, Public Health Scotland, and NHS Assure. The health board continues to face considerable challenges in its pursuit of reducing emissions and achieving net zero status. A significant obstacle is the high cost associated with transitioning to renewable energy sources and implementing sustainable practices across various sectors, especially during a period marked by financial constraints within the organisation. Pension Provision: The pension provision is calculated using information received from the Scottish Public Pension Agency (SPPA) relating to former NHS Grampian employees for whom NHS Grampian have an ongoing pension liability. Estimation of the liability to pay pensions for these staff depends on a number of complex judgements relating to the discount rates used, the rate at which salaries are projected to increase, changes in retirement ages, mortality rates and expected returns on pension fund assets. Reliance is placed on significant details provided by the actuary of the Pension Fund to establish the value of this liability. NHS Grampian was not able to set a balanced revenue budget for the 2024/25 financial year. The financial plan submitted to Scottish Government in March 2024 projected a £59.1 million deficit for the year after forecasting savings of £34.9 million. Scottish Government confirmed additional funding for Health Boards across the year of £17 million, also cost pressures of £22.8 million of which £21.4                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                           | [29]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           29 |
| Grampian-2024-2025 | Q_2025_SAV_04    | How much of the 2024/25 efficiency savings were non-recurring?                                                                                | [31]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2024-2025:p0031_001       | programme of schemes which identify efficiencies that have a financial benefit, enabling the Board to achieve and maintain financial balance through effective management, control, innovation, quality and best practice. The main savings achieved in addition to technical adjustments (£28.6m) were in the areas of agency spend including medical locum and nursing costs, hospital drug costs from switching from branded medicines to generic alternatives, staff cost control measures including vacancy freeze and reduction of overtime, procurement and increased charges for catering. Recurring savings amounted to £25.2 million (2023/24 £12.38 million) and non-recurring of £25.3 million (2023/24 £5.14 million). 3.2.3 Expenditure Analysis Net expenditure for the year was £1,681 million (2023/24 £1,565 million), an increase of £116 million from the prior year. The scale of these savings was significantly higher than in the previous year and the delivery of these savings was supported by the development of an assurance and performance framework. A Programme Management Office is in place to support this along with other elements of value and sustainability work. The NHS Grampian Value and Sustainability Framework was set up in 2022 to support the delivery of a comprehensive programme of schemes which identify efficiencies that have a financial benefit, enabling the Board to achieve and maintain financial balance through effective management, control, innovation, quality and best practice. | Grampian-2024-2025 | Q_2025_AE_01     | How did the volume of unscheduled emergency care demand compare to the previous year across NHS Grampian in 2024/25?                          | [14]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2024-2025:p0009_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW Summary of Key Issues Affecting Overall Delivery - Enduring levels of high pressure across the system which impact on the need to re- prioritise capacity and reduce ability to release time to engage with core service delivery has been a significant challenge. - System pressures continue to impact on hospital flow and delivery of Unscheduled Care standards which is leading to increased planned care backlog and waits. Despite much committed and hard work by our staff, deterioration has occurred as evidenced by performance in relation to delayed discharges, 4 hour Emergency Department waits and Treatment Time Guarantee waits, although cancer treatment times have largely been stable. - Ability to balance meeting agreed financial performance whilst delivering planned and urgent/unscheduled care standards together and responding to sustained and unprecedented operational pressures across the system remains an ongoing risk. The ambitious saving targets in place for this financial year have been met. - Workforce, finance, and system pressures in general continue as enduring risks affecting NHS Grampian’s ability to maintain momentum towards delivery of our identified strategic objectives. 5. | Grampian-2024-2025 | Q_2025_DEL_P1    | What are the three strategic pillars of the NHS Grampian Delivery Plan 2024-2027?                                                             | [7]              | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | A OVERVIEW                    | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          0 | FP2_MISSED_TOP_RANK | retrieval       |                    |                          | Grampian-2024-2025:p0062_000       | A CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT In support of this, a Three Year delivery plan 2024-2027 was agreed by the Board as part of a single coherent plan including the Annual Delivery Plan and Medium Term Financial Plan for NHS Grampian. This Delivery plan sets out how NHS Grampian will make progress against the priority areas and strategic intent within the Plan for the Future for 2024 – 2027 in response to the 10 recovery priority areas of the Scottish Government. The Delivery Plan 2024 – 2027 was formally adopted by the Board in June 2024, approved by Scottish Government by letter dated 28 May 2024. The Plan for the Future strategic intent will be delivered through a rolling programme of priority projects and actions, with defined milestones, articulated in a five year rolling Delivery Plan. The Medium Term Financial Framework details the financial position for the next 5 years, reflects our route map to recovering the deficit position. As a result of the continued significant operational pressures being experienced across the health and social care system, plus the high level of inflation, it will not be                                                                                                                                                                                            | [62]        | ACCOUNTABILITY REPORT              | A) CORPORATE GOVERNANCE REPORT                                                                 |           62 |
| Grampian-2024-2025 | Q_2025_STR_01    | What is the period covered by the NHS Grampian Delivery Plan adopted in June 2024?                                                            | [7]              | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | A OVERVIEW                    | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2024-2025:p0007_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT A OVERVIEW NHS Grampian’s Delivery Plan which was adopted by the Board in June 2024. NHS Grampian Delivery Plan April 2024 – March 2027. The Delivery Plan covering the period April 2024 to March 2027 has been developed jointly with our three Health and Social Care Partnerships, colleagues, citizens, and wider partners. The plan contained our objectives and key priorities. The agreed objectives over the period April 2024 to March 2027 were:  Strengthen colleague and citizen engagement to improve health (People)  Create the conditions for sustainable change (Places)  Improve preventative and timely access to care (Pathways) Our objectives and priority areas for delivery during 2024–2027 are framed around the key components of our Strategy (‘ Plan for the Future 2022-28 ’), People, Places and Pathways and the 10 Scottish Government Drivers for Recovery. A high level outline of the priorities aligned to the organisation’s strategy and the Scottish Government recovery drivers is illustrated overleaf. NHS Grampian was unable                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                        | [7]         | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | A) OVERVIEW                                                                                    |            7 |
| Grampian-2024-2025 | Q_2025_ESC_01    | At what point in the Scottish Government's oversight hierarchy was NHS Grampian positioned at the start of 2025, and what triggered the move? | [30]             | PERFORMANCE REPORT    | B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS        | paragraph         | []                    | {'scope_doc_id': True, 'scope_year': True, 'prefer_evidence_layout': 'paragraph', 'prefer_table_chunks': False} |          1 | HIT                 | none            |                    |                          | Grampian-2024-2025:p0030_000       | PERFORMANCE REPORT B PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS NHS Grampian has been receiving support from the Scottish Government since November 2023, when the Board was escalated to Stage 2 of the Scottish Government’s Support and Intervention Framework. Further escalation took place in January 2025, when NHS Grampian was escalated to Stage 3 of the framework based on the deterioration in the financial position of the Board during 2024/25 and the recommendation of the National Planning and Performance Oversight Group. Senior officers from within NHS Grampian continue to work closely with the Scottish Government to identify and implement solutions to return the Board to financial balance. In 2024/25 NHS Grampian has also been escalated from Stage 2 to Stage 3 of the Scottish Government’s Support & Intervention Framework because of the Board’s financial position and have benefited from additional support and scrutiny. Escalation to Stage 4 was confirmed on 12 May 2025 due to financial sustainability and the deterioration of the Board’s financial position during 2024/25; and Leadership and Governance, it will bring enhanced scrutiny and support from the Scottish                                                                                                                                                 | [30]        | PERFORMANCE REPORT                 | B) PERFORMANCE ANALYSIS                                                                        |           30 |
